---
title: "EEG-ECMR Progress"
author: "Jordan Gunn"
date: last-modified
number-sections: true
filters:
  - collapse-callout
collapse-callout:
  all: true
callout-appearance: simple
callout-icon: true
format: html
---

This document records our progress and key decisions as we build a brain-based version of eCMR using EEG data from Zarubin et al. (2020).

Right now, we are making a simple prototype to demonstrate item-level LPP signals (an EEG measure) can improve a retrieved context model's predictions of the emotion boost to memory.

Later, we may try other model designs, fitting methods, and ways to evaluate the results.

# Data Preparation

The primary table comes from `Single_Trial_Behavioural_and_EEG_Data_Z.csv` located at `Z:\Talmi lab space\Archive\Secondary_Data_Analysis_Zarubin_From_Robin\Manuscript\files from robin nov24\`. 

This was missing some study events and their recall results. So I merged it with the behavioral log `All_Included_Subjects.csv` located at `Z:\Talmi lab space\Archive\Secondary_Data_Analysis_Zarubin_From_Robin\Data\Behaviour\Behaviour_csv_files\`. This file has recall data for every non-buffer item but no LPP scores. Before merging, I confirmed no data present in the EEG file disagreed with the behavioral log. For example, no items marked as recalled in the EEG file were marked as not recalled in the behavioral log.

The merged frame contains 6,840 study events (342 participant × list trials, 20 items per list after trimming the two primacy buffer items). 
Among these, 371 study events (5.42 %) lacked EEG measurements.
The gaps affected both valence conditions: negative lists contributed 125 of 2,266 events (5.52 %; mean 0.37 ± 0.65 items per trial), whereas neutral lists contributed 246 of 4,574 events (5.38 %; mean 0.72 ± 0.93 items per trial). 
Overall, 202 of 342 lists (59.1 %) needed at least one interpolated observation, with 1.84 +/- 1.01 missing items among impacted trials (median 2; maximum 6).
Subject-level exposure ranged from 2 to 19 items (3.9 to 10 % of each participant’s 180 study events), so the imputation step is methodologically substantive.

LPP amplitudes were interpolated hierarchically. 
For study events still missing LPP scores, I filled values using each person's average for items of the same emotion in the same trial. 

Each trial originally had 22 items. 
The first two were removed during preprocessing to reduce primacy effects and cannot be retrieved. 
This is not ideal for CMR-style models, which often use early items to shape context. 
For now, we will just ignore the missing items, treating each list as having 20 items.

The dataset does not include recall order. 
It only marks whether each item was recalled. 
Because of this, even early prototypes require specifying custom loss functions for model fitting that do not rely on recall order information.

# Benchmark Analyses

These checks do two things. They verify that data preparation worked. They also set clear targets for model development. Unless noted, error bars show 95% bootstrapped confidence intervals across participants. We may switch to hierarchical (subject and trial level) confidence intervals later to better account for data structure.

<!-- TODO: Switch to a hierarchical confidence interval over subjects *and* trials. -->

## Recall Rate by Study Position and Item-Condition / Category-SPC

Our main behavioral check is the category serial position curve. It appears in the “Meeting with Nathaniel Daw” slides on the lab drive. For each position, we compute the share of items recalled among items with that category label.

::: {#fig-reference_catspc layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/daw_meeting_slide5.png)

![](results/figures/analyses/reference_catspc.png)

Recall rate by study position and item type from reference materials (LEFT) and from the present dataset (RIGHT).
:::

The close match between the shared and regenerated curves builds confidence that data preparation worked as intended.

## LPP by Study Position and Item-Condition

These plots help us see how LPP relates to study position and emotion. Most ways of adding LPP to eCMR will aim to make the category-SPC differences look more like the differences seen here.

::: {#fig-cat_lpp_spc layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/analyses/cat_lpp_spc_EarlyLPP.png)

![](results/figures/analyses/cat_lpp_spc_LateLPP.png)

LPP amplitude by study position and item type for Early LPP (LEFT) and Late LPP (RIGHT).
:::

Both Early and Late LPP vary with emotionality across positions. Still, error bars are large and mean values swing widely across positions.

## Recall Rate by LPP and Item-Condition

A useful neural add-on to eCMR should go beyond labeling items as emotional or neutral. It should also use LPP to predict recall within each label. To confirm that LPP relates to recall, we bin items by LPP amplitude and plot recall rate within each bin.

::: {#fig-cat_recall_by_early_lpp layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/reference_cat_recall_by_lpp.png)

![](results/figures/analyses/cat_recall_by_lpp_EarlyLPP.png)

Recall rate by Early LPP amplitude and item type in reference materials (LEFT) and in the present dataset (RIGHT).
:::

<!-- 
> Figure 3: I don't understand why your figure is different from robin's. To clarify Robin's figure, this is not raw data - it's data from the mixed model which will have removed some random effects. The early LPP values you used may be raw, while his may be z-scores? 

I am confident that Robin and I both used the same Z-normalized LPP values. However, Robin's figure represents lines of best fit from a mixed effects model predicting recall from LPP and item type, while my figure shows directly observed recall rates within percentile-defined LPP value bins. The fitted model uses observations across the full range of LPP values to estimate recall rates at value bands with few observations, while my binned approach estimates recall rates only from items within each bin. Therefore, the two figures differ in how they estimate recall rates at extreme LPP values with few observations.

I believe my figure would align more with Robin's if I used model predictions rather than raw recall rates, or if I improved binning to balance counts across bins. However, I think the best path forward is configure analyses of the relationship between LPP and recall that avoid binning entirely, as in my LPP by Recall Status and Item-Category plots below. If your concern is focused more on whether my data practices match Robin's, I can take steps to confirm this by replicating his mixed effects model analysis and plotting procedure. 

For reference, Robin included a note about the the Z-transformation applied to LPP values in a corresponding README file:

> Z indicates that the amplitudes have been z-normalised. Please note that this normalisation is done by separately for every trial and electrode and involves subtracting the baseline (-200-oms) from each data point after 0 and dividing by the SD of the -200-0ms time window. In other words it is similar to a standard ERP baseline correction with the only difference of dividing with the SD of the baseline. The z normalisation gives change in amplitude from baseline on a z-scale where M = 0 and SD = 1.
 -->

::: {#fig-cat_recall_by_late_lpp}
![](results/figures/analyses/cat_recall_by_lpp_LateLPP.png){width="50%"}

Recall rate by Late LPP amplitude and item type.
:::

::: {#fig-cat_recall_by_late_lpp layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/analyses/cat_recall_by_lpp_EarlyLPP_counts.png)

![](results/figures/analyses/cat_recall_by_lpp_LateLPP_counts.png)

Counts of observations per bin for Early LPP (LEFT) and Late LPP (RIGHT).
:::

<!-- 
> Figure 5: I don't understand how it's possible to have so many more occurrences of LPP for neutral items than negative ones at every level of LPP and still have higher memory/LPP for negative?

There are more neutral items than negative items in the dataset overall, which leads to higher counts in each LPP bin for neutral items. However, recall rates are computed as the proportion of recalled items within each bin, so higher counts do not directly translate to higher recall rates. 

Once we account for this count imbalance, we still observe 1) that LPP amplitudes for negative items tend to be higher on average than for neutral items and 2) that recall rates scale more strongly with LPP amplitude for negative items than for neutral items.
 -->

An earlier bug set subject bin values to 0 when a bin had no items. That lowered recall estimates for sparse bins. After fixing this, we see a positive link between LPP and recall, with wide intervals at high amplitudes due to low counts.

Still, this approach for visualizing the relationship between LPP and recall seems flawed because of differences in the number of observations available in each bin. There are few cases in very high LPP bins above 4.86 and very low bins below -3.25. Error bars help show uncertainty, but the uneven counts make it hard to interpret the trends. A future revision may either set bin edges to balance counts or otherwise drop binning entirely. Still, we preserve this analysis for comparison against a similar plot from prior work. Similarities in trends help confirm that our data preparation worked as intended.

<!-- TODO: Balance the number of observations per bin by refactoring implementation of procedure from Morton & Polyn (2016). -->

## LPP by Recall Status and Item-Category
To examine the link between LPP and recall without binning, we plot LPP for recalled and unrecalled items within each category.

::: {#fig-cat_lpp_by_recall_split layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/analyses/cat_lpp_by_recall_EarlyLPP_Negative.png)

![](results/figures/analyses/cat_lpp_by_recall_EarlyLPP_Neutral.png)

![](results/figures/analyses/cat_lpp_by_recall_LateLPP_Negative.png)

![](results/figures/analyses/cat_lpp_by_recall_LateLPP_Neutral.png)

LPP amplitude by recall status and item type for Early LPP (TOP row) and Late LPP (BOTTOM row), split by NEGATIVE items (LEFT column) and NEUTRAL items (RIGHT column).
:::

::: {#fig-cat_lpp_by_recall_combined}

![](results/figures/cat_lpp_by_recall.png){width="50%"}

LPP amplitude by recall status and item type for both Early LPP. For clarity, lines across item types and recall status are shown together and confidence intervals are omitted.
:::

These plots suggest that higher LPP is tied to recall for NEGATIVE items, with no clear link for NEUTRAL items. LPP also cannot distinguish unrecalled NEGATIVE items from either recalled or unrecalled NEUTRAL items.

The pattern is clearer for Early LPP than for Late LPP, which supports focusing on Early LPP in the model. The pattern also suggests that we only use LPP to modulate recall for NEGATIVE items, leaving NEUTRAL items unaffected.

## Key Carried-Over Results

::: {#fig-amplitude_by_cat_by_recalled}
![](results/figures/amplitude_by_cat_by_recalled.png)

LPP by time, recall status, and item type.
:::

::: {#fig-reference_gmm}
![](results/figures/reference_gmm.png)

Description of a generalized mixed effects model predicting recall as a function of condition, earlyLPP, and their interaction.
:::

These plots from prior work on this project motivate our choice to focus on Early LPP, to use LPP only for NEGATIVE items, and to treat the relationship between EarlyLPP and recall rate for NEGATIVE items as roughly linear in initial model designs.

# Loss Functions For Model Fitting

We face two main challenges when defining loss functions for this dataset. First, models like CMR and eCMR predict the order of recalls, but our data only say whether each item was recalled. This forces a simpler question: does the set of recalled items match what the model predicts?

Second, the first two items of each list were removed from the data. This helps reduce primacy effects for emotion analyses, but it removes useful information for modeling how lists were encoded and retrieved.

Together, these issues prevent standard likelihood tests on recall sequences. For now, we ignore the missing items and fit models to predict the set of recalled and unrecalled items that we do observe. We also skip modeling how many items were recalled in each trial. CMR and eCMR do not make strong counts predictions without extra rules for stopping recall. Dropping recall counts keeps the task focused on which items were recalled rather than how many or in what order.

## Set Likelihood

The best way to evaluate models is to compute the likelihood of the observed data under each model. We normally do this by comparing predicted and observed recall sequences. But our data lack recall order information.

Without recall order, we must rate models by the probability they give to the set of recalled items. Many recall orders can lead to the same set. A complete set likelihood function would thus compute and sum the probabilities of all recall sequences that produce the observed set. This is possible in theory, but it is very costly in practice. The number of possible recall sequences grows factorially with the number of recalled items. Summing over all sequences quickly becomes infeasible.

A Monte-Carlo approach sidesteps this problem by evaluating only a sample of possible recall sequences.
But there are many ways to implement this idea.
The approach I first considered focused sampling on simulations from the model.
For each subject and trial, we can simulate many recall sequences. We can then check how many simulated sequences match the observed set of recalled items. The proportion of matching sequences gives an unbiased estimate of the set likelihood given the model and parameter set.

The problem with this approach is that it often gives zero likelihood to reasonable parameter sets. If no simulated sequence matches the observed set exactly, the estimated likelihood is zero. This is especially likely when many items are recalled, since the number of possible recall sequences grows quickly with the number of recalled items. Even when some simulated sequences match the observed set, the estimate can be noisy unless many simulations are run per subject and trial. This makes fitting costly and slow.

I'm now exploring a cleaner approach. Instead of sampling model simulations of a given trial, we can sample permutations of the observed set of recalled items. Each permutation is a possible recall sequence that produces the observed set. For each permutation, we can compute its likelihood under the model. Aggregating these likelihoods gives an estimate of the set likelihood.

This approach avoids zero likelihoods for reasonable parameter sets, since every permutation of the observed set has some non-zero likelihood under the model. It also supports Monte-Carlo estimation to control cost, since we can choose how many permutations to sample per subject and trial. Finally, it preserves the advantages of likelihood-based model fitting and comparison over alternatives like mean squared error (MSE). Once initial prototyping is done, I'll implement this approach for model fitting and evaluation.

## Mean Squared Error (MSE) Over Summary Statistics

Given initial challenges implementing a set-likelihood-based fitting procedure, we are starting with a simpler approach for prototyping. We instead use mean squared error between a chosen summary statistic in the data and the same statistic from simulations. This is common in retrieved-context model evaluation and fits our constraints.

The procedure is simple. We choose a summary statistic from the experiment. We simulate the model with a parameter set. We compute the same statistic on the simulations. We then compute the mean squared difference between the two numbers.

This score tells us how well a parameter set for the model reproduces the pattern we care about. If the best score is still poor, the model is likely not adequate. Fitting by MSE does not find the maximum-likelihood parameters and loses trial-level detail, but it is practical, scalable, and clear.

We currently use the category serial position curve for model fitting and evaluation. It computes recall by position separately for negative and neutral items. This tests both position effects and the emotion boost at the same time and is a better target for neurally informed eCMR.

The MSE can compose over multiple statistics if needed. We may add a neural-behavioral link statistic later to ensure the model captures LPP-recall relationships. For now, we keep it simple. And we expect to switch back to likelihood-based fitting anyway once basic prototypes work.

# Modeling

We start with standard CMR, which ignores emotion and LPP. This baseline checks that the emotion boost to memory does not appear without additional mechanisms.

Next, we build and select between simple versions of eCMR that can capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect. Our candidate models focus on the proposal that the boosted recall for emotional items over neutral items comes from extra attention during study, boosting the strength of context-to-item associations for emotional items relative to neutral items. We set aside other possible mechanisms of the emotion-enhanced memory effect for now, such as boosted integration rates for emotional or temporal context, or enhanced item-to-context learning for emotional items. Many model variants are still possible within this attention-focused framework. We explore some issues below.

To preserve the interpretability of downstream extensions and avoid overfitting, we focus on simple eCMR variants that use as few extra parts as necessary. We only add what is needed to capture available behavioral benchmarks, especially the emotion-enhanced memory effect. We also check that these models cannot capture the link between LPP and recall without directly using LPP data.

Once a simple eCMR specification is chosen, we add a neural extension that uses item-level LPP to scale the strength of emotional attention. This model has two goals. It should beat both baseline CMR and the simple eCMR on the emotion effect. It should also capture how LPP relates to recall for both emotional and neutral items. Meeting both goals will give us a solid starting model that we can improve later with better data and stronger techniques for model fitting and evaluation.

The remainder of this section is structured around modeling decisions. Each subsection describes a dilemma we faced, the choice we made, the reasoning behind it, and results from testing it.

## Single vs Dual Context Representations

Full eCMR adds a separate emotional context and memories that link emotion to items and back to context. In that setup, attention mainly raises the learning rates in those emotion links. At recall, reinstating the emotional context then favors emotional items.

A simpler specification may be enough to capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect in our data. This specification matches baseline CMR and has only one context (temporal). This version skips emotional context and memories linking emotion to items and back to context. An emotional-attention parameter directly scales the strength of temporal context-to-item links during study. Just one extra parameter beyond baseline CMR controls this scaling. We call this version **pure-attention eCMR**.

<!-- 
> pure-attention eCMR: I didn't know how to 'tell' the model that an item was emotional without telling it that the context of the item was emotional. how is this solved here?

Rather than 'tell' the model that an item is emotional, pure-attention eCMR posits that the only difference between how emotional and neutral items are encoded for later recall is how much attention they receive during study. Emotional items receive more attention, which strengthens the associations between temporal context and those items. This differentiation works similarly to how CMR scales context-to-item associations based on primacy effects, where early list items receive more attention and thus stronger associations.
 -->

An alternative simplification called **two-context eCMR** keeps two contexts but but fixes the emotional context to fully track the most recent item. That removes the need to learn emotion feature to context links or configure an emotional context integration rate. Emotional context-to-feature memory is maintained with a emotional-attention parameter that sets how strongly emotional context cues emotional items. Thus, like pure-attention eCMR, this version only adds one parameter beyond baseline CMR.

<!-- 
> two-context eCMR: it's not clear here which context-to-item parameter is increased with emotion, temporal or emotional. 

In two-context eCMR, the learning rate for emotional context-to-item associations (not temporal context-to-item associations) is scaled up for emotional items. This means that when emotional context is reinstated during recall, it more strongly cues emotional items due to the enhanced associations formed during encoding.

> "fixes the emotional context to fully track the most recent item" - what parameter is fixed here?

The drift rate ($\beta_{enc}$) specifically for emotional context is set to 1 during encoding. This means that emotional context fully updates to reflect the features of the most recently studied item, effectively making it a direct representation of the last item's emotional features. This is in contrast to standard eCMR, where emotional context can reflect a recency-weighted history of the emotional features of multiple prior items. 
 -->

Pure-attention eCMR says attention directly strengthens context-to-item links. The two-context version says emotional items help reinstate emotional context, which then favors other emotional items. Both models thus propose that attention during study drives the emotion effect, but the two-context version explicitly proposes that emotional items cluster together at recall via emotional context reinstatement. We cannot compare these proposals directly because the present dataset lacks recall order. Even so, this model can still make different predictions about the composition of recalled items for lists with mixed emotional and neutral items. We can at least compare their ability to capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect.

### Parameter Sensitivity Tests

::: {#fig-two_context_emcr_parameter_sensitivity layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_emotion_scale.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_emotion_scale.png)

Sensitivity of serial position curve to changes in the emotional-attention scaling parameter in Two-Context eCMR. Left: NEGATIVE items. Right: NEUTRAL items.
:::

::: {#fig-attention_emcr_parameter_sensitivity layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_emotion_scale.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_emotion_scale.png)

Sensitivity of serial position curve to changes in the emotional-attention scaling parameter in pure-attention eCMR. Left: NEGATIVE items. Right: NEUTRAL items.
:::

<!-- 
> Figures 10-11: " The Two-Context eCMR variant appears to produce a bigger boost to recall rates for the first and last study positions for negative items" - I didn't see this in the graph

You are right. My comments were about an older version of the plots where differences were apparent. I'll update the text to remove that claim. Instead, I think the broad conclusion is that both models are hard to distinguish at all based on our comparisons in this document.
 -->
These results show that scaling up the parameter increases recall rates for negative items while reducing recall rates for neutral items, building confidence that the parameter can improve the baseline model's ability to address the emotion-enhanced memory effect.
Both model variants show similar sensitivity to changes in the emotional-attention scaling parameter, despite their different mechanisms. 
This suggests that either model could potentially capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect with appropriate parameter settings.

### Comparison of Fits to Benchmark Statistics

#### Negative + Neutral Category SPC

::: {#fig-two_context_vs_attention_cat_spc layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

Category-SPC for NEGATIVE and NEUTRAL items combined. Left column: Two-Context eCMR. Right column: Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::

::: {#fig-baseline_mcr_cat_spc}

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_WeirdCMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png){width="50%"}

Recall rate by study position and item type for Baseline CMR.
:::

#### Negative-Category SPC

::: {#fig-two_context_vs_attention_cat_spc_negative layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

Category-SPC for NEGATIVE items. Left column: Two-Context eCMR. Right column: Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::

#### Neutral-Category SPC

::: {#fig-two_context_vs_attention_cat_spc_neutral layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

Category-SPC for NEUTRAL items. Left column: Two-Context eCMR. Right column: Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::

#### Overall Serial Position Curve

::: {#fig-two_context_vs_attention_spc layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

Overall serial position curve across item types. Left column: Two-Context eCMR. Right column: Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::


#### LPP by Recall Status and Item-Category

It's also possible to examine how well pure-attention eCMR captures the relationship between LPP amplitude and recall probability for emotional and neutral items.
This sets a higher bar for model performance, since the model must not only capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect but also the graded relationship between LPP and recall probability within each item category.

::: {#fig-attention_emcr_cat_recall_by_early_lpp layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_EARLYLPP.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEUTRAL_EARLYLPP.png)

Early LPP by recall status for NEGATIVE items (LEFT) and NEUTRAL items (RIGHT) under Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::

<!-- 
> 4.1.2.5 here you seem to use LPP in the model but how did you do this? where is it discussed? I didn't understand what's plotted in fig 17. might be useful to say 'simulated recall rate' in figures that are from models, like in fig 17 (which otherwise looked too much like fig 6) and also in fig 20 and 29. Sorry I'm being thick; I didn't immediately understand why LPP (a measured number) changes when you change model parameters. Later, I thought it's because the model changes recall rates at each SPC; this could be stated in the legend - simulated recall rate at each SPC - just as a reminder.

I did not use LPP in these models. LPP values at each study event do not change model behavior at all in pure-attention eCMR or two-context eCMR. I generated this plot anyway by simulating each subject's trials (study and recall events) using the model and parameters fitted to those subjects. I then plotted the relationship between studied items' LPP values and whether those items were recalled in the model's simulations. This helps us see whether the model can capture the *observed* interactive relationship between emotionality, LPP, and recall, even without using LPP as an input. The plots show that they cannot. Despite reproducing an emotion-enhanced memory effect, neither model reproduces the observed relationship between LPP and recall within each item category. This outcome is key because it motivates the need for a neurally-informed extension that captures this relationship.
 -->


### Discussion
Neither model makes substantially better predictions of the emotion-enhanced memory effect as measured by category-SPC than the other. Both models improve substantially over baseline CMR, which cannot capture the effect at all. For now, we default to using pure-attention eCMR as our base model because of its relative simplicity. If initial neural extensions do not help, we can revisit this choice later. (In practice, both models are usually evaluated, but reports in this document focus on pure-attention eCMR unless the two-context variant shows a different pattern of results.)

<!-- 
> 4.1.3 "Both models struggle to capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect adequately. Most critically, they underestimate recall rates for negative items past study position 10. They also overestimate recall rates for neutral items, particularly in early study positions between 3 and 8." I didn't see this in the graph

You are right. Those sentences were based on an earlier version of the plots where these discrepancies were apparent. After some further tuning, I was happily able to eliminate those specific issues. I'll update the text to remove those claims. 
-->

## Additive vs Multiplicative Interaction Between Primacy and Emotional Attention

<!-- 
> 4.2 please add equations to clarify what emotion_scale does. Good point about multiplicative effects. I think I naively thought: if a neutral item is attended at level x (whether primacy, or not) I will make the emotional one 2x. That what would matter is that you always boost the emotional item to the same degree relative to what it would have been. I didn't care if in SPC4 emotion boost is 3x and SPC 1 30x, because this preserved the relationship of 10x and 1x for neutral. I didn't notice that there was a special misfitting to the early positions. Is it possible that because I only used the emotion boost for the emotional context, and the primacy is for temporal context, I didn't see this issue?

In the specifications and code I've seen for both eCMR and CMR3, primacy was applied to both emotional context- AND temporal context-to-item learning rates. So I think it's more likely that the multiplicative interaction is easier to overlook when hand-fitting parameters than when using an automated fitting procedure. On the other hand, it's also possible that a multiplicative interaction between primacy and emotional attention is actually a really good model, and that this dataset is atypical in showing a misfit in early positions. This possibility is especially salient given that we lack recall data for the first two list items in each trial, which limits our ability to measure primacy effects directly. In any case, I'll support these model specification sections with equations going forward to make the implementation decisions clearer.

TODO: Provide equations for all model variants discussed in this document.
 -->

The specifications for eCMR and CMR3 both seem to configure a multiplicative relationship between emotional-attention and primacy-attention.
That means their effects combine by multiplying rather than adding.
If primacy attention is high at the start of the list and the emotion scale is also high, the total boost for an early emotional item is large.
For a late item where primacy is low, the same emotion scale gives a much smaller boost. This predicts a larger gap between emotional and neutral recall early in the list than later.

Here is an example. Suppose primacy at position 1 equals 10 and the emotion scale equals 3. The boost for an emotional item is 10 times 3 which is 30. The boost for a neutral item is 10. The gap is 20. At a later position where primacy equals 1, the emotional boost is 3 and the neutral boost is 1. The gap is 2. The early gap is ten times larger.

Our data and current pipeline give only a limited test of this question because we lack recall order and recall data for the first two list items in each trial. Still, we can still compare additive and multiplicative fits to recall rates by position and category. Initial tests apply an MSE-based fit to a simple extension of baseline CMR where emotional items get either a multiplicative or additive boost to context-to-item associations during encoding (pure-attention eCMR).

::: {#fig-additive_vs_multiplicative_cat_spc layout-ncol="3"}

![](results/figures/reference_catspc.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStopMultiplicative_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

Recall rate by study position and item type for data (LEFT), Additive Pure-Attention eCMR simulation (MIDDLE), and Multiplicative Pure-Attention eCMR simulation (RIGHT).
:::

We find that the additive variant better captures the emotion-enhanced memory effect without over-predicting recall rates for negative items in early study positions. This question can be revisited later with better data, more powerful fitting methods, and more complex models. For now, we assume an additive relationship between primacy and emotional attention.

Since two-context eCMR has two context-to-item memory structures (temporal and emotional), it can implement additive, multiplicative, or no relationship between primacy and emotional attention in each memory structure. If we assume that emotional attention is always applied in emotional-context-to-item learning and primacy is always applied in temporal-context-to-item learning, this specifies 3 x 3 = 9 possible relationships. A couple of these have been evaluated, all finding that keeping emotional attention and primacy independent works best. Thus, for two-context eCMR we also assume an additive/independent relationship between primacy and emotional attention.

## Neurally-Informed Extension

<!-- 
> 4.3 please add equations. Please also explain about the threshold - not sure what that does, and why.

I'll add equations to clarify the implementation of the neurally-informed extension. I think I can go a little further in explaining the threshold here as well.

TODO: Add equations and further explanation of the threshold parameter.
 -->

We want our neurally-informed eCMR to use item-level Early LPP data to both:

1. Improve model fits to the emotion-enhanced memory effect; and
2. Capture how LPP relates to recall within each item category.

To constrain the design of this extension, we lean on the linearity assumptions embodied in earlier generalized mixed effects model results. The model predicted recall from item emotionality and single trial Early LPP amplitude, finding strong effects of both predictors and their interaction. Recalled negative items had higher Early LPP than unrecalled negative items, while neutral items showed little difference. From the GLMM results, we make two working assumptions. First, LPP affects recall for emotional items in a roughly linear way. Second, the context to feature learning rate configures recall rate.

We thus design a simple neurally-informed eCMR extension that uses Early LPP to scale the emotional-attention parameter for emotional items only. Neutral items remain unaffected. We use a `lpp_slope` parameter to set how strongly Early LPP spreads recall rates apart for emotional items. An `lpp_threshold` parameter replaces `emotion_scale` from earlier variants and sets the cutoff between recalled and unrecalled emotional items. Depending on whether Early LPP is above or below this threshold, emotional attention increases or decreases relative to the neutral baseline.

This slope and threshold approach gives a clear, data grounded starting point for testing approaches for relating LPP to memory in eCMR. After it works, we can explore nonlinear features if we want.

### Parameter Sensitivity Tests

#### Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR

::: {#fig-eeg_attention_emcr_cat_spc_parameter_sensitivity layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_lpp_slope.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_lpp_slope.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_lpp_threshold.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_lpp_threshold.png)

Sensitivity of negative-category (LEFT column) and neutral-category (RIGHT column) serial position curve to changes in the `lpp_slope` (TOP row) and `lpp_threshold` (BOTTOM row) parameters in Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::

::: {#fig-eeg_attention_emcr_negative_lpp_by_recall layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_UNRECALLED_shift_lpp_slope.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_RECALLED_shift_lpp_slope.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_UNRECALLED_shift_lpp_threshold.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_RECALLED_shift_lpp_threshold.png)

Sensitivity of Early LPP by recall status for NEGATIVE items to changes in the `lpp_slope` (TOP) and `lpp_threshold` (BOTTOM) parameters in Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR. Left: UNRECALLED items. Right: RECALLED items.
:::

#### Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR

::: {#fig-eeg_twolayer_emcr_cat_spc_parameter_sensitivity layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_lpp_slope.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_lpp_slope.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_lpp_threshold.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_lpp_threshold.png)

Sensitivity of serial position curve to changes in the `lpp_slope` (TOP) and `lpp_threshold` (BOTTOM) parameters in Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR. Left: NEGATIVE items. Right: NEUTRAL items.
:::

::: {#fig-eeg_twolayer_emcr_negative_lpp_by_recall layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_UNRECALLED_shift_lpp_slope.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_RECALLED_shift_lpp_slope.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_UNRECALLED_shift_lpp_threshold.png)

![](results/figures/shifting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_RECALLED_shift_lpp_threshold.png)

Sensitivity of Early LPP by recall status for NEGATIVE items to changes in the `lpp_slope` (TOP) and `lpp_threshold` (BOTTOM) parameters in Neurally-Informed eCMR. Left: UNRECALLED items. Right: RECALLED items.
:::

### Comparison of Fits to Benchmark Statistics

#### Negative + Neutral Category SPC

::: {#fig-eeg_attention_emcr_cat_spc_comparison}
![](results/figures/reference_catspc.png){width="50%"}

Recall rate by study position and item type from reference materials.
:::

::: {#fig-eegemcr_cat_spc layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

Recall rate by study position and item type for Regular/Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR (TOP LEFT/RIGHT), and Regular/Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR (BOTTOM LEFT/RIGHT).
:::

#### Negative-Category SPC

::: {#fig-eegemcr_cat_spc_negative layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

Recall rate for NEGATIVE items by study position, for Regular/Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR (TOP LEFT/RIGHT), and Regular/Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR (BOTTOM LEFT/RIGHT).
:::

#### Neutral-Category SPC

::: {#fig-eegemcr_cat_spc_neutral layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

Recall rate for NEUTRAL items by study position, for Regular/Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR (TOP LEFT/RIGHT), and Regular/Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR (BOTTOM LEFT/RIGHT).
:::

#### Regular Serial Position Curve

::: {#fig-eegemcr_spc layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

Recall rate for ALL items by study position, for Regular/Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR (TOP LEFT/RIGHT), and Regular/Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR (BOTTOM LEFT/RIGHT).
:::

<!-- 
> Fig 26, all 4 panels look the same, I am guessing the two models are the same, neurally-informed did nothing?

Yes, my apologies for not adding commentary in the text. The neurally-informed extensions did not improve fits to category-specific or general serial position curves compared to their regular eCMR counterparts. This outcome suggests that while the neurally-informed models help capture how LPP relates to recall at the item level, they do not enhance the models' ability to fit the overall emotion-enhanced memory effect as measured by serial position curves. 

It is hard to assess how surprising this result is, roughly for the reasons I've already enumerated in the Discussion section.

- The GMM results don't necessarily imply that adding LPP data will improve fits to summary statistics like category-SPC. They don't show that LPP gives us much more information about overall recall rates for emotional vs neutral items than we already get from simply using the labels. Instead, the GMM results mainly show that LPP can help us distinguish which emotional items are more likely to be recalled.

- Our neurally informed extensions are designed to capture the relationship between LPP and recall at the item level in the same way that the GMM does. The `lpp_threshold` parameter is fitted to capture the average boost in attention received by emotional items compared to neutral items. It thus directly addresses the emotion-enhanced memory effect in practically the same way as the `emotion_scale` parameter configuring emotional attention in regular eCMR. I think we confirmed resoundingly that the emotion-enhanced memory effect can be captured by an additive emotional attention mechanism. 

- On the other hand, the `lpp_slope` parameter is not really designed to configure differences in overall recall rates between emotional and neutral items at all. Instead, it attempts to capture variability in emotional attention between items as a function of their LPP values. It can shift around _which_ emotional items are recalled or not, but it does not necessarily change _how many_ emotional items are recalled overall compared to neutral items. Again, this is the outcome we should have expected coming in based on the GMM results, which show that LPP relates to recall within emotional items but not neutral items.

So as long as we 1) are assessing models based on their ability to predict how much more frequently emotional items are recalled compared to neutral items, and 2) stick to GMM-style hypotheses about the relationship between LPP and recall, we should therefore not expect the neurally-informed extensions to outperform regular eCMR variants. We will only find differences when we assess models based on their ability to predict which specific emotional items are recalled or not, which is the next step in our modeling pipeline.
 -->

#### Early LPP by Recall Status and Item-Category

::: {#fig-eegecmr_lpp_by_recall_reference}

![](results/figures/analyses/cat_lpp_by_recall_EarlyLPP_Negative.png){width="50%"}

LPP amplitude by recall status for NEGATIVE items from reference materials.
:::

::: {#fig-eegecmr_lpp_by_recall layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_EARLYLPP.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_2EEGAttentionECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_EARLYLPP.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_SimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_EARLYLPP.png)

![](results/figures/fitting/TalmiEEG_2EEGSimpleTwoLayerECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_EARLYLPP.png)

Early LPP by recall status for NEGATIVE items, for Regular/Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR (TOP LEFT/RIGHT), and Regular/Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR (BOTTOM LEFT/RIGHT).
:::

# Discussion and Next Steps

<!-- 
> Remaining steps: 1 makes sense; perhaps the fit will look more obvious when it's not about the SPC , although the current approach made sense to me. Still, as no evidence was found, we need to show that we didn’t find it even when we used the most rigorous approach. 

I think it's maybe misleading to frame the difference between the two approaches as a matter of rigor. Either can be appropriate depending on the modeling goals and data available, so neither is inherently more rigorous than the other.

The current approach makes _the most_ sense if your goal is to assess whether LPP provides additional information about overall recall rates for emotional vs neutral items beyond what eCMR already captures. But if you have the broader objective to assess whether adding LPP data to eCMR improves its ability to explain why certain items are recalled or not in a given trial, then the current approach is misaligned with that goal.

Of course, I do think the broader objective is more valuable for building psychological theory about how LPP and emotion relate to memory. 

> “Refocus evaluation on the complete eCMR specification” – that also made, because we don’t have data here to evaluate which eCMR version is better, might as well use what worked before, unless we have a reason not to – although it made sense to me also to use the simplest version.

For now, I still think we should seek the simplest model that does the job. Since both pure-attention and two-context eCMR variants are special cases of the complete eCMR specification, any successes we find with those simpler models should generalize to the complete specification. If we find that neither simpler variant can capture the data adequately, then we can revisit the complete specification later. I don't think we've yet reached that point!
 -->

Our goals for this stage of our modeling were twofold. First, we wanted to build a simple eCMR variant that could capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect in our data. Second, we wanted to extend that model to use item-level Early LPP data to both improve fits to the emotion effect and capture how LPP relates to recall within each item category.

In this stage of the project, we focused on two benchmark statistics: the category-split serial position curve and the relationship between Early LPP and recall for negative items. We used mean squared error between simulated and observed statistics as our loss function for model fitting and evaluation. We compared baseline CMR, two simple eCMR variants (pure-attention and two-context), and neurally-informed extensions of those eCMR variants. We found that both eCMR variants but not baseline CMR could capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect to a reasonably high degree. The neurally-informed extensions primarily additionally gained the ability to capture the relationship between Early LPP and recall for negative items, with small or no improvements to fits to the emotion effect. We thus partially met both our goals for this stage of the project.

Our most ambiguous result is that none of the presented eCMR variants, not even neurally-informed extensions, clearly outperformed the other when fitting the behavioral emotion-enhanced memory effect. This outcome probably reflects three realities:

- **Limited information in LPP signal**. Early LPP data captures differences in recall rate for emotional items. However, the category-split serial position curve measures differences in recall rate between emotional and neutral items. Thus, Early LPP data may not directly inform fits to that statistic. It is best suited for configuring predictions about which emotional items are most likely to be recalled, rather than how emotional items differ from neutral items overall.
- **Lack of theoretical constraint in order-free data and summary statistics**. The primary difference between pure-attention and two-context eCMR is in their predictions about how emotional items cluster together in recall sequences. Two-layer eCMR predicts that boosted recall for emotional items is contingent on reinstating emotional context that cues other emotional items. Pure-attention eCMR predicts that emotional items are recalled more often regardless of whether the last recalled item was emotional or neutral. Without recall order data, we cannot test this key theoretical difference. Both models can make similar predictions about overall recall rates for emotional and neutral items, making it hard to distinguish them.
- **Informal model fitting and evaluation procedure**. Our current fitting procedure uses mean squared error between simulated and observed summary statistics. We primarily compare models by visually inspecting fits to those statistics. Even if we directly compared final MSE scores, differences in the number of parameters per model and the lack of formal likelihoods would hamper strong conclusions.

We aren't necessarily interested in selecting between possible eCMR specifications with this project. Instead, our goal is to point the way toward neurally-informed retrieved-context models of emotional memory. But as is, we haven't clearly shown that adding LPP data to eCMR helps capture behavioral patterns of recall beyond what eCMR can already do.

While some of these problems cannot be overcome with the present dataset, others can probably be resolved by shifting the focus of model fitting and evaluation. We can directly optimize and test models for their ability to predict (give higher likelihoods) to the specific items recalled or not recalled in each trial. This shift would better align our modeling goals with the signal we understand LPP to provide. It would also better distinguish between eCMR variants by comparing their ability to predict trial-level data rather than summary statistics and enabling formal comparison tests that account for differences in model complexity. 

We *know* that LPP relates to recall at the item level, and that pure-attention and two-context eCMR variants give different probability distributions over possible recall sets of mixed emotional and neutral item lists. But our current benchmarks do not directly test these relationships. Moving to a trial-level, likelihood-based approach for model fitting and evaluation should help clarify whether adding LPP data to eCMR improves its ability to capture emotional memory effects, and whether this dataset is useful for distinguishing between eCMR variants more generally.

So here are the remaining steps as I see them:

1. Swap to a set-likelihood-based fitting and evaluation procedure as described earlier. Redo model fitting with this new procedure and simulate summary statistics with the best-fitting parameters.
2. Compare model fits to both summary statistics and trial-level set likelihoods. Check whether adding LPP data improves eCMR's ability to capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect and whether trial likelihoods help distinguish between eCMR variants after accounting for model complexity.
3. Optional: Refocus evaluation on the complete eCMR specification, contrasting it against a neurally-informed extension and against baseline CMR. Unnecessary if goal is just to prove a neurally-informed baseline.
4. Optional: Refine bootstrapped confidence intervals for summary statistics to better capture uncertainty in model predictions. Unnecessary since current approach already matches standard practice and other results (e.g., GMM outcomes) already confirm existing patterns.
5. Write up results for internal review and eventual publication, Methods-focused talk, grant materials, etc.

<!-- 

So my plan for this document?

Tell a clear story about addressing the behavioral effect without a neurally informed model or likelihood-based fitting.
Then show how the neurally informed model fits into the story, but makes likelihood-based loss salient.
Then show how the likelihood-based fitting procedure can help us distinguish between models and better capture the behavioral effect.

What is the story without a neurally informed model or likelihood-based fitting?
Sets baseline. We have an effect. We have this model. How can we assess whether the model captures the effect? Answer: MSE-based fitting to summary statistics. Baseline CMR can't do it. If you let attention/learning rates vary by emotion, you can capture the effect. Can stick to just pure-attention eCMR for this story.

Then bring in LPP data, describing its complex relationship with recall. Propose a neural linking hypothesis based on GMM results and eCMR: that LPP and emotion interact to configure emotional attention during encoding. So again. We have an effect, this time about LPP. We have a model. How can we assess whether the model captures the effect? 

First we tackle with the same MSE-based approch as before. Key result: we can now predict a relationship between LPP and recall within emotional items. This is the chief object of a neurocognitive model: to express a concrete and testable mechanistic account of the link between neural activity and behavior. However, LPP doesn't help much with the emotion effect since our linking hypothesis suggest LPP configures differences in recall between emotional items, not in recall between emotional and neutral items overall.

This result is dissatisfying because we expect a neurocognitive model to allow us to predict behavior better than a purely cognitive model when neural data is on hand. But to assess this properly, we need to shift to a likelihood-based fitting procedure that better aligns with our modeling goals. We switch from asking whether a model can capture a specific pattern in free recall to asking whether a model can capture which specific items are recalled or not in each trial. This shift better aligns with our modeling goals and the signal LPP provides. We redo model fitting with this new procedure and simulate summary statistics with the best-fitting parameters. Most importantly, we compare the likelihoods of the data under each model, accounting for differences in model complexity.

Hmm, this is maybe too complicated a story.
 -->
